tasks.overarching_goal: Before we simulate the complex diverging Smith-Purcell Radiation (SPR), we must validate the custom ray-tracing engine. We are doing this by constructing a standard, textbook 4-grating Treacy compressor. The goal is to prove the engine can accurately calculate spatial recombination of different wavelengths.
tasks.task_description: Perform these two actions: 1.) Delete all the current optics under the build_system function. Start with nothing 2.) Add a simple piece of code to the program (if it isn't already there), that when you run the program if there are no optics created, the optical simulation will print a string saying No optics have been added or something like that. This is just so the user knows that they need to add optics.
tasks.handoff_description: Look over the build_system function and determine if all optics inside have been properly deleted. There should be no optics at the moment. If the deletion of the optics wasn't successfull then delete them yourself. Also, make sure the proper code has been written that tells the user once the simulation is run that the
tasks.section: Verify Physics by building a 4 grating compressor
Verify Physics by building a 4 grating compressor
tasks.task_description: Create a single source under the build_system framework. Then make sure the source logic is fleshed out. What I would like to see is the ability to define a generic source of light with input variables defined as # of rays, wavelengths, and propagation direction. The Once the source architecture can be implemented for the start of the ray trace, the # of rays I want to use will be 3. I want the wavelengths to be 9.5, 10, and 10.5 um, and I want them to be initially all superimposed on top of each other traveling in the +y-direction (orientiation_angle=90 deg). Define the starting position or source position as (0,0).
tasks.handoff_description: Critically review that a single source of light was implemented properly. Determine if 3 rays were inputted with wavelengths of 9.5, 10, 10.5 um, and the light will be traveling in the +y-direction (orientation_angle=90 deg). If the initial setup of the source is incorrect please fix it. Secondly, check the source code logic is sound. The source should emitted all at the same time, and the rays should be all pointing in the same direction. If the code is attempting to implement a time delay framework cause it thinks the source is an SPR source it should be ok still. But if there is a simple fix, without changing the underlying ray trace framework, try to resolve it.
tasks.overarching_goal: Before we simulate the complex diverging Smith-Purcell Radiation (SPR), we must validate the custom ray-tracing engine. We are doing this by constructing a standard, textbook 4-grating Treacy compressor. The goal is to prove the engine can accurately calculate spatial recombination of different wavelengths.

tasks.section: Verify Physics by building a 4 grating compressor
tasks.overarching_goal: Before we simulate the complex diverging Smith-Purcell Radiation (SPR), we must validate the custom ray-tracing engine. We are doing this by constructing a standard, textbook 4-grating Treacy compressor. The goal is to prove the engine can accurately calculate spatial recombination of different wavelengths.
tasks.task_description: I want to define a 4 grating compressor using 4 gratings all of the same groove period. Let's use 100 lines/mm, and 2 in. diameter for all gratings, and make the clear aperture just equal to the diameter for now as well. To start the radiation from the source will be traveling from (0,0) upwards in the xy-plane. I want to define 4 grating positions in a similar fashion to the picture attached under image_path. The light from the source should be hitting the 1st grating at some acute incident angle, then the light should be (in much the same way as the imaage provided) angularly dispersed in space according to the diffraction equation for a grating that is already set in the engine. The light that is angularly dispersed travels to the 2nd grating. Make sure the 2nd grating's face is parallel with the 1st grating's face a given distance away. The separation distance between the 2 gratings can be defined for the time being as 100 mm away. So you will have to determine where to place the 2nd grating so the light angularly dispersed off the 1st grating makes its way to the 2nd grating. Then, the light is recollimated but now with a spatial chirp. It hits a 3rd grating that has the negative orientation_angle of the 2nd grating and then the light is angularly dispersed in such a way that it starts to refocus toward the 4th grating. The 4th grating face is parallel to the 3rd grating's face in much the same way as gratings 1 and 2, and the orientation_angle of the 4th grating should be the negative of grating 1. If done correctly, the output of the beam should be recompressed (zero spatial chirp) as shown in the image provided. Lastly, make sure thata the simulation time span is not set too long. We only need to simulate the propagation of the source of light until just after the light diffracts off the final grating.
tasks.handoff_description: Critically review that all 4 gratings were implemented with the same groove period, diameter, and clear aperture. Further, determine if the grating positions were centered properly with respect to the incoming beam. The beam from the defined source should be transported through all 4 gratings to the final one where it is recompressed (in much the same way as the provided image). Check to make sure the time span of the simulation is not set too long. We only need to simulate the propagation of the source of light until just after the light diffracts off the final grating. If ANY of the things is off, please make the necessary changes to correct the simulation behavior.
